disease.name: Peptic Ulcer Disease
disease.note: Erosion of the gastric or duodenal mucosa to the submucosa caused by H. pylori, NSAIDs, or acid hypersecretion. Complications (bleeding, perforation, obstruction) require surgical intervention. In Kenya, H. pylori is highly prevalent and NSAIDs from over-the-counter use are common causes. Perforated ulcers present late and carry high mortality.
presentingComplaints: Burning pain in the upper abdomen
Pain that wakes you up at night
Pain that is better after eating or with antacids
pathophysiologyDetail: H. pylori pathogenesis: Gram-negative spiral bacterium colonizes gastric mucus → urease converts urea to ammonia → neutralizes acid → permits survival in acidic environment → CagA and VacA cytotoxins damage epithelial cells → chronic active gastritis → ulceration

NSAID-induced ulcer: NSAIDs inhibit cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) and COX-2 → reduced prostaglandin synthesis (PGE2, PGI2) → decreased mucus secretion, bicarbonate production, and mucosal blood flow → impaired epithelial restitution and repair → mucosal erosion to ulceration

Duodenal ulcer acid hypersecretion: H. pylori infection of gastric antrum → increased gastrin release from G-cells → parietal cell hyperplasia → increased basal and stimulated acid output → high acid load delivered to duodenal bulb → gastric metaplasia → H. pylori colonization of metaplastic epithelium → ulcer formation

Gastric ulcer pathogenesis: different from duodenal — usually associated with atrophic gastritis, hypochlorhydria, or normochlorhydria; impaired mucosal defense more than acid hypersecretion; NSAIDs and bile reflux play larger roles

Forrest classification of bleeding ulcers: Ia (spurting hemorrhage), Ib (oozing), IIa (non-bleeding visible vessel), IIb (adherent clot), IIc (flat pigmented spot), III (clean base) — predicts rebleeding risk from 55% (Ia) to <5% (III)

Perforation pathophysiology: full-thickness ulcer erosion through muscularis propria → free perforation releases gastric/duodenal contents into peritoneal cavity → chemical peritonitis → bacterial peritonitis (E. coli, Klebsiella, anaerobes) → sepsis → multi-organ failure

Penetrating ulcer: erosion into adjacent organs (pancreas most common, also liver, biliary tree, gastrocolic ligament) → contained perforation with inflammation but no free air → severe referred pain (back pain in pancreatic penetration)

Zollinger-Ellison syndrome: gastrin-secreting neuroendocrine tumor (gastrinoma) → massive gastrin release → extreme acid hypersecretion → severe, refractory, and often multiple peptic ulcers in atypical locations (post-bulbar, jejunal) → associated with MEN-1

Stress ulcer pathophysiology: ICU patients with major burns (Curling ulcer), head injury (Cushing ulcer), shock, sepsis, multi-organ failure → splanchnic hypoperfusion → mucosal ischemia → reperfusion injury → oxidative stress → ulcer formation within 24 hours

H. pylori-induced hypergastrinemia: antral H. pylori infection impairs D-cell function → reduced somatostatin production → loss of negative feedback on G-cells → hypergastrinemia → parietal cell hyperplasia → increased acid output in duodenal ulcer patients

Mucosal defense mechanisms: pre-epithelial (mucus-bicarbonate barrier — 95% HCO3, mucin glycoproteins), epithelial (tight junctions, rapid restitution, PGE2, heat shock proteins), subepithelial (microvascular blood flow, sensory afferent nerves, mast cells)

Helicobacter species other than H. pylori: H. heilmannii (5% of infections, milder gastritis), H. bilis (biliary tract), H. cinaedi (immunocompromised) — may cause ulcer but less common in East Africa

Gastric metaplasia in duodenum: response to high acid load → duodenal epithelium transforms to gastric-type epithelium → allows H. pylori colonization → ulcer develops at the metaplastic patch — explains why duodenal ulcers occur at the duodenal bulb

East African context: H. pylori seroprevalence 70-90% in rural Kenya; NSAID overuse from over-the-counter access; perforated ulcer accounts for 15-20% of acute surgical admissions; delayed presentation (>24 hours) common; HIV co-infection alters ulcer healing and increases perforation risk

H. pylori eradication failure: increasing clarithromycin resistance in East Africa (15-25%) due to widespread macrolide use for respiratory infections; bismuth-based quadruple therapy or levofloxacin-based rescue therapy may be needed

Recurrent ulcer after surgery: if incomplete vagotomy (positive sham-feeding test), retained gastric antrum (after Billroth II), Zollinger-Ellison, or persistent H. pylori infection — always investigate the cause of recurrence

landmarkTrials.trial: Marshall and Warren (1984, The Lancet) — H. pylori Discovery
landmarkTrials.population: Barry Marshall ingested H. pylori culture to fulfill Koch's postulates
landmarkTrials.intervention: Self-ingestion of H. pylori culture — developed acute gastritis, then cleared with antibiotics
landmarkTrials.outcome: Established H. pylori as causative agent of gastritis and peptic ulcer. Nobel Prize in 2005. Revolutionized ulcer treatment from 'no acid, no ulcer' to 'no H. pylori, no ulcer'.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: H. pylori eradication now standard in Kenya; test-and-treat policy for dyspepsia recommended

landmarkTrials.trial: H. pylori Eradication in Duodenal Ulcer (1993, New England Journal of Medicine — Hentschel et al)
landmarkTrials.population: 104 patients with H. pylori-positive duodenal ulcer
landmarkTrials.intervention: Ranitidine + triple therapy (bismuth, metronidazole, tetracycline) vs ranitidine alone
landmarkTrials.outcome: Ulcer healing at 6 weeks: 92% vs 75%. Ulcer recurrence at 12 months: 8% vs 86% (p<0.001). H. pylori eradication virtually eliminated recurrence.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Landmark proof that H. pylori eradication prevents recurrence — foundation of modern ulcer management

landmarkTrials.trial: PPI vs H2RA for Bleeding Ulcer (1990s meta-analysis — Lau et al)
landmarkTrials.population: Multiple RCTs comparing high-dose PPI vs H2RA or placebo after endoscopic hemostasis
landmarkTrials.intervention: High-dose IV PPI (omeprazole 80 mg bolus + 8 mg/hr) vs placebo/H2RA for 72h after endoscopic hemostasis
landmarkTrials.outcome: PPI reduced rebleeding (RR 0.40, 95% CI 0.28-0.59), need for surgery (RR 0.43), and mortality (RR 0.41). Number needed to treat to prevent rebleeding: 6.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: High-dose IV PPI protocol standard in Kenyan hospitals — omeprazole on essential medicines list

landmarkTrials.trial: Endoscopic Hemostasis for Bleeding Ulcer (1999, The Lancet — Lau et al)
landmarkTrials.population: 100 patients with actively bleeding ulcers or visible vessels, randomized to endoscopic therapy vs sham
landmarkTrials.intervention: Adrenaline injection + thermal coagulation (heater probe) vs sham endoscopy
landmarkTrials.outcome: Rebleeding: 8% vs 27% (p=0.01). Emergency surgery: 6% vs 24% (p=0.02). Mortality: 2% vs 8% (p=0.20, nonsignificant trend). Combined endoscopic therapy superior to single modality.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Endoscopic hemostasis available at Kenyan tertiary centers; adrenaline injection alone effective when thermal/clips unavailable

landmarkTrials.trial: Graham Patch vs Laparoscopic Repair for Perforated Ulcer (2005, Annals of Surgery — Laparoscopic vs Open)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 15 studies comparing laparoscopic vs open repair of perforated peptic ulcer
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic omental patch repair vs open patch repair
landmarkTrials.outcome: Laparoscopic: less pain, shorter hospital stay (5 vs 8 days), fewer wound infections. Higher recurrence of leak (RR 2.4) — but not statistically significant. Conversion rate 12%. Open preferred for large ulcers >10 mm and shock.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Open repair standard in Kenya where laparoscopic expertise limited; open is safe and effective

landmarkTrials.trial: PPI vs Surgery for Perforated Ulcer (Boey Score Validation — 1987, British Journal of Surgery)
landmarkTrials.population: Boey et al, 275 patients with perforated duodenal ulcer — developed Boey scoring system
landmarkTrials.intervention: Simple patch repair with or without definitive ulcer surgery
landmarkTrials.outcome: Boey score predictors: (1) major medical illness, (2) preoperative shock, (3) perforation >24h. Mortality: 0 factors → 0%, 1 factor → 5%, 2 factors → 15%, 3 factors → 45%. Validated worldwide.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Boey score highly relevant in Kenya where delayed presentation is common; guides triage for ICU vs ward

landmarkTrials.trial: Helicobacter pylori Eradication in NSAID Users (1998, The Lancet — Chan et al)
landmarkTrials.population: 202 H. pylori-positive NSAID users without prior ulcer
landmarkTrials.intervention: H. pylori eradication (bismuth, tetracycline, metronidazole) vs placebo before NSAID course
landmarkTrials.outcome: Ulcer at 8 weeks: eradication 12% vs placebo 34% (p=0.003). H. pylori eradication reduced NSAID-associated ulcer risk. Combining eradication + PPI even more effective.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: H. pylori test-and-treat recommended for all Kenyan patients starting NSAID therapy

landmarkTrials.trial: Second-Look Endoscopy in Bleeding Ulcer (2009, The Lancet — Chiu et al)
landmarkTrials.population: 194 patients with high-risk bleeding ulcers (Forrest Ia-IIb) after successful endoscopic hemostasis
landmarkTrials.intervention: Routine second-look endoscopy at 16-24h vs clinical observation
landmarkTrials.outcome: No significant difference in rebleeding (7% vs 9%), surgery, or mortality. Selective second-look based on clinical judgment recommended over routine practice.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: In resource-limited settings, selective second-look conserves endoscopy capacity for new cases

landmarkTrials.trial: Bismuth Quadruple Therapy for H. pylori (2007, Alimentary Pharmacology — Cochrane Review)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of H. pylori eradication regimens
landmarkTrials.intervention: Bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI + bismuth + metronidazole + tetracycline) vs standard triple therapy
landmarkTrials.outcome: Quadruple therapy: 87% eradication vs 78% for triple therapy (p<0.001). Superior in clarithromycin-resistant strains. First-line in areas with high clarithromycin resistance.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Relevant for Kenya where clarithromycin resistance is rising (15-25%); bismuth available

evidenceTable.therapy: PPI for healing duodenal ulcer (4 weeks)
evidenceTable.effect: 80-95% healing with PPI vs 50-70% with H2RA
evidenceTable.recommendation: PPI first-line for all active ulcers
Standard first-line if local clarithromycin resistance <15%

evidenceTable.therapy: Bismuth quadruple therapy for resistant H. pylori
evidenceTable.recommendation: First-line in high clarithromycin resistance areas (including Kenya)

evidenceTable.therapy: High-dose IV PPI after endoscopic hemostasis
evidenceTable.population: Bleeding peptic ulcer after endoscopic therapy
evidenceTable.recommendation: Standard post-endoscopic therapy for high-risk stigmata

evidenceTable.therapy: Adrenaline injection + thermal coagulation for bleeding ulcer
evidenceTable.recommendation: Combination therapy superior to single modality

evidenceTable.therapy: Graham omental patch repair for perforated ulcer
evidenceTable.effect: Mortality 5-10%; leak rate 2-5%; Boey score predicts mortality

evidenceTable.therapy: H. pylori eradication before NSAID therapy
evidenceTable.effect: 12% vs 34% (RR 0.35) — eradication reduces NSAID ulcer risk
evidenceTable.recommendation: Test and treat H. pylori before starting NSAIDs

evidenceTable.therapy: Maintenance PPI vs H2RA for ulcer prevention
evidenceTable.recommendation: PPI maintenance therapy for high-risk patients
Use Boey score for risk stratification and triage

evidenceTable.therapy: Proton pump inhibitor vs H2RA for prevention of stress ulcers in ICU
evidenceTable.population: ICU patients at risk of stress ulcer bleeding
evidenceTable.recommendation: PPI first-line for stress ulcer prophylaxis in ICU

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical diagnosis — epigastric pain with meal relationship (duodenal: 2-4 hrs after, relieved by food; gastric: worsened by food), night pain awakening, NSAID history, H. pylori exposure risk factors

Step 2: Upper GI endoscopy (EGD) — gold standard with biopsy. Document ulcer location (gastric vs duodenal), size, depth, Forrest classification for bleeding. Gastric ulcer: biopsy ulcer edge (minimum 6-8 biopsies) to exclude malignancy

Step 3: H. pylori testing — 13C-urea breath test or stool antigen test (preferred over serology). Biopsy-based rapid urease test (CLO test) if endoscopy performed. Test before starting PPI as PPI suppresses H. pylori and causes false negatives

Step 4: If perforation suspected — erect chest X-ray (free air under diaphragm: 70-85% sensitivity). If CXR negative but high suspicion: CT abdomen with oral contrast (98% sensitivity for extraluminal air)

Step 5: If upper GI bleeding — assess hemodynamic stability (Rockall score, Glasgow Blatchford score). Start high-dose IV PPI before endoscopy. Urgent endoscopy (<12 hours for high-risk, <24 hours for low-risk)
Step 6: Laboratory — CBC, coagulation profile, LFTs, BUN/Cr (BUN/Cr ratio >20 suggests upper GI bleeding). Cross-match if active bleeding. Amylase to rule out pancreatitis in atypical presentations
Step 7: CT abdomen — for complicated ulcers: evaluate for contained perforation, penetration into pancreas, abscess formation, or alternative diagnoses (pancreatitis, aortic aneurysm)

Step 8: Surgical evaluation if — failed endoscopic hemostasis, recurrent bleeding, perforation, gastric outlet obstruction, suspicion of malignancy, or large gastric ulcer not healing after 8-12 weeks of PPI therapy

Step 9: Zollinger-Ellison workup — if multiple ulcers, post-bulbar ulcers, refractory to therapy, diarrhea, family history of MEN-1. Check fasting gastrin level (off PPI x 1 week if possible), secretin stimulation test, somatostatin receptor scintigraphy

Step 10: Follow-up endoscopy — gastric ulcers must have repeat endoscopy at 8-12 weeks to confirm healing and biopsy any residual ulcer to exclude malignancy. Duodenal ulcers do not require routine follow-up endoscopy

Kenya adaptation: In many district hospitals, clinical diagnosis + response to PPI trial is pragmatic; endoscopy referral for alarm symptoms or non-response; H. pylori stool antigen testing available at most sub-county hospitals
managementPearls: All gastric ulcers must be biopsied (minimum 6 biopsies from ulcer edge and base) and followed up with repeat endoscopy at 8-12 weeks to confirm healing — 2-5% of benign-appearing ulcers are malignant

H. pylori test-and-treat is cost-effective in Kenya: 14-day triple therapy (PPI + amoxicillin 1g BD + clarithromycin 500 mg BD) eradicates 80-90%; rising clarithromycin resistance means bismuth quadruple therapy should be considered as first-line in recurrent cases

For bleeding ulcers, high-dose IV PPI (omeprazole 80 mg bolus + 8 mg/hr infusion) for 72 hours after successful endoscopic hemostasis reduces rebleeding by 60% (NNT 6) — this is the single most important medical therapy after endoscopy

Endoscopic therapy for bleeding ulcers: combination therapy (adrenaline 1:10,000 injection around the bleeding point + thermal coagulation or clips) is superior to any single modality; adrenaline alone without second modality has higher rebleeding

Forrest classification is critical: Forrest Ia-Ib (active bleeding) and IIa (visible vessel) require endoscopic therapy; IIb (adherent clot) requires endoscopic irrigation and therapy of underlying vessel; IIc (flat spot) and III (clean base) can be managed medically

Perforated peptic ulcer: Boey score is the best prognostic tool — preoperative shock, major medical illness, and perforation >24 hours predict mortality (0% → 45%); score guides triage: low score → laparoscopic repair, high score → open repair ± ICU

Graham omental patch repair is the standard of care for perforated duodenal ulcer — do NOT add a definitive acid-reducing procedure (vagotomy) in the emergency setting due to increased leak risk and operative time

NSAID-induced ulcers need definitive NSAID cessation — alternative analgesia (paracetamol, tramadol, topical NSAIDs) should be arranged; if aspirin is required for cardiovascular protection, add lifelong PPI co-therapy
H. pylori eradication should be confirmed with a 13C-urea breath test or stool antigen test at least 4 weeks after completing therapy (off PPI for 2 weeks) — stool antigen is more practical in Kenya

Stress ulcer prophylaxis in ICU: PPI (omeprazole 40 mg IV daily or orally via NG) is preferred over H2RA; indicated for mechanical ventilation >48 hours, coagulopathy, burns >30% BSA, head injury, shock, or history of PUD
Cigarette smoking delays ulcer healing (by 30-50%), increases recurrence, and increases perforation risk — smoking cessation counseling is as important as pharmacotherapy in ulcer management

In perforated peptic ulcer with Boey score 2-3 (high mortality), consider damage control surgery: simple patch repair with abdominal lavage, close, and plan for re-look laparotomy if needed; avoid prolonged surgery in unstable patient
operativeNuances: Graham patch repair: approach via upper midline laparotomy; locate the perforation (commonly anterior duodenal bulb); mobilize the duodenum (Kocher maneuver) to identify posterior wall perforation if present; freshen ulcer edges by gentle debridement

Omental patch harvest: use a pedicled piece of greater omentum — ensure adequate length without tension; place the omental plug INTO the perforation (not over it) and secure with 3-0 silk sutures placed full-thickness through healthy duodenal wall 5 mm from the ulcer edge

Suture technique for Graham patch: place the first suture at 12 o'clock position, then 3, 6, and 9 o'clock; take generous bites (8-10 mm) of healthy duodenal wall — the tissues are edematous and sutures may cut through if too shallow or tied too tightly

Laparoscopic Graham patch: consider for stable patients with Boey score 0-1 and perforation <10 mm; 3-port technique (umbilical camera 30°, left and right subcostal working ports); suture repair with intracorporeal knotting — requires advanced laparoscopic skills

For large duodenal perforations (>2 cm) that cannot be closed primarily: use a pedicled jejunal serosal patch (Roux-en-Y onlay) or a pyloric exclusion with gastrojejunostomy — these complex repairs have higher morbidity and should only be attempted by experienced surgeons

Thorough peritoneal lavage is critical in perforation: use 5-10 L warm normal saline; lavage all quadrants until returns are clear; pay particular attention to the right upper quadrant (subphrenic space) and pelvis where pus collects; leave a drain in the hepatorenal pouch (Morrison's pouch)

Under-running a bleeding duodenal ulcer: make a longitudinal duodenotomy in the second part of the duodenum; identify the bleeding vessel (usually gastroduodenal artery branch at the posterior duodenal bulb); place three figure-of-eight 3-0 silk sutures — above, at, and below the bleeding point

In Kenya, where advanced laparoscopic equipment and critical care resources may be limited, open Graham patch repair through a midline laparotomy is the safest and most reliable approach — it is well-established, quick (<45 minutes), and requires minimal specialized equipment

Definitive ulcer surgery (vagotomy and antrectomy or highly selective vagotomy) is rarely performed today due to excellent medical therapy; reserve for Zollinger-Ellison syndrome, recurrent bleeding despite eradication, or patient unable to take PPI lifelong

Postoperative antimicrobials in perforation: coverage for gram-negative (E. coli, Klebsiella), anaerobes (Bacteroides), and enterococci; 3rd-generation cephalosporin + metronidazole for 5-7 days; duration depends on degree of contamination at surgery

prognosis.uncomplicatedUlcerEradication: >90% ulcer healing with PPI + H. pylori eradication; <5% recurrence at 1 year if H. pylori eradicated and NSAIDs avoided
prognosis.bleedingUlcerEndoscopicTherapy: Rebleeding rate: 5-15% after endoscopic hemostasis + high-dose PPI; 80-90% avoid surgery; mortality 5-10% (higher in elderly, comorbid, in-hospital bleeds)
prognosis.perforatedUlcer: Overall mortality 5-15%; Boey score: 0 factors → <1%, 1 → 5%, 2 → 15%, 3 → 45%; delayed presentation >24h doubles mortality
prognosis.nsaidUlcerCessation: Discontinuation + PPI: 80-90% healing at 8 weeks; if NSAID continued: 40-50% healing at 8 weeks despite PPI
prognosticFactors: H. pylori eradication status — single most important predictor of long-term cure
NSAID continuation — continued use of NSAIDs/aspirin dramatically increases recurrence and complications
Age >65 — increased risk of bleeding, perforation, and mortality from complications
Co-morbid illness (Boey score) — cardiac, renal, hepatic failure increase surgical mortality
Time to presentation in perforation — each hour delay increases mortality; >24 hours = poor prognosis
Forrest classification — Ia/Ib/IIa have high rebleeding risk; IIc/III have excellent prognosis
Hemodynamic stability on presentation — shock at presentation predicts poor outcomes
Ulcer size >2 cm — higher risk of failed medical therapy, perforation, and malignant potential (gastric)
prognosis.eastAfricanSpecific: Perforated ulcer in Kenya: median time to presentation 36-48 hours (vs 12 hours in high-income countries); Boey score 2-3 in 40% of patients; mortality 15-20% in published Kenyan series; H. pylori eradication rate 75-85% with standard triple therapy; increasing clarithromycin resistance necessitates stewardship

history_questions.question: Do you have a burning or gnawing pain in the upper abdomen?
Is the pain better or worse after eating?
Does the pain wake you up at night?
Do you take antacids or omeprazole for the pain?
Do you use ibuprofen, diclofenac, aspirin, or other painkillers?
Do you take traditional herbal medicines for pain?
Have you noticed black, tarry stools?
Have you vomited blood or coffee-ground material?
Do you smoke cigarettes?
Do you drink alcohol — chang'aa, busaa, or beer?
Have you ever been tested or treated for H. pylori?
Do you drink a lot of tea, coffee, or soda?
Have you had a sudden severe pain that made you unable to move?
Do you feel bloated or full after small meals?
Have you had an endoscopy before?
Has anyone in your family had stomach ulcers?
Are you under a lot of stress?
Have you lost weight recently?
Do you eat a regular diet or skip meals often like ugali with sukuma wiki?
Do you have a history of TB infection or have you taken TB treatment? (INH can cause ulcers)
Have you ever had an ulcer before? How was it treated?
Do you take steroids (prednisone, dexamethasone) for any reason?
Do you feel that the pain moves to your back? (suggests penetrating ulcer)
Have you had an episode where you passed out or felt very dizzy? (hypovolemia from bleeding)
Do you get pain in your shoulder tip? (referred phrenic irritation — perforation sign)
Are your symptoms worse during certain seasons? (ulcers may flare seasonally)
Do you have a family history of pancreatic or kidney tumors? (MEN-1/Zollinger-Ellison)
Do you have chronic watery diarrhea? (Zollinger-Ellison syndrome)
Do you know your HIV status? (HIV increases ulcer risk and complication rates)
Have you had surgery on your stomach before for an ulcer?
Do you chew khat (miraa)? (associated with increased gastric acid)
Do you have chronic kidney disease? (uremic gastropathy)
Do you take iron supplements or other medications that may irritate your stomach?
Have you been treated for H. pylori before and did you complete the full course?
Do you drink a lot of tea (chai) with milk and sugar? (may contribute)
Do you feel pain when your stomach is empty and get relief immediately after eating? (duodenal ulcer classic)
Do you ever vomit blood that looks like coffee grounds or bright red blood?
Have your stools recently become black and sticky like tar?

examination_findings.finding: Epigastric tenderness to palpation
Guarding or rigidity (if perforation) — board-like abdomen
Pallor from anemia (acute or chronic blood loss)
Tachycardia from bleeding or pain
Hypotension from significant bleeding
Melena on digital rectal examination (DRE)
Hematemesis observed or reported
Generalized peritonitis if perforation — rebound, guarding, rigidity
Absent bowel sounds if perforation with ileus
Fever if perforation with peritonitis
Succussion splash if gastric outlet obstruction
Epigastric mass if penetrating ulcer or abscess
Signs of hypovolemia: cold peripheries, delayed capillary refill
Hepatic dullness loss on percussion (if perforation — free air)
Guarding and rigidity — board-like abdominal wall in perforation
Generalized abdominal tenderness with rebound and percussion tenderness
Fever — present in perforation with established peritonitis
Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/hr) from hypovolemia from bleeding or third-spacing in peritonitis
Jaundice — rare, suggests penetrating ulcer into pancreas or bile duct
Epigastric bruit (rare) — suggests ulcer penetration into gastroduodenal artery
Palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier sign) if penetrating ulcer obstructs bile duct
Back tenderness on fist percussion over costovertebral angle — suggests retroperitoneal perforation
Abdominal distension from ileus in peritonitis or gastric outlet obstruction
Absent bowel sounds — late sign in peritonitis from perforation
Septic shock: hypotension unresponsive to fluids, mottled skin, altered mental status, oliguria
Shoulder tip pain (Kehr sign) on palpation — suggests diaphragmatic irritation from perforation
Coffee-ground or bloody NG aspirate confirms upper GI bleeding
Melena on DRE — confirms upper GI bleeding if stools not yet passed
Psoas sign — suggests retrocecal or perforation with retroperitoneal involvement
Obturator sign — suggests pelvic abscess from perforation

bedside_scores.name: Rockall Score (Bleeding Ulcer)
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score 0-2: low risk rebled; 3-5: moderate; ≥6: high risk — consider ICU

bedside_scores.name: Glasgow Blatchford Score (Pre-endoscopy)

components.name: BUN, Hb, SBP, HR, melena, syncope, liver/CCF history

bedside_scores.interpretation: Score 0: low risk, outpatient management. Score ≥1: need endoscopy.

riskFactors.note: Most important cause — 70-90% of duodenal ulcers
Including ibuprofen, diclofenac, aspirin — common OTC in Kenya
Especially chang'aa and busaa — direct mucosal irritant
Physiologic stress in ICU patients — stress ulcer prophylaxis indicated
Gastrinoma causing massive acid hypersecretion — rare

subtypes.name: Duodenal Ulcer
subtypes.management: PPI therapy (Omeprazole 20 mg daily) for 4-8 weeks. H. pylori triple therapy (PPI + Amoxicillin 1g + Clarithromycin 500 mg, both BD for 14 days). If NSAID-induced: stop NSAID, PPI. Surgical referral for complications.

subtypes.name: Gastric Ulcer
subtypes.management: PPI therapy. H. pylori eradication if positive. Upper endoscopy with biopsy of all gastric ulcers to exclude malignancy. Follow-up endoscopy in 8 weeks to confirm healing. Surgical referral for refractory cases or complications.

mimics.whyMimic: Epigastric pain, anorexia, weight loss, anemia
Severe epigastric pain out of proportion to exam, vomiting, possible GI bleeding
Epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting, diaphoresis — mimics perforation
Sudden severe epigastric pain, hypotension, shock — mimics perforated ulcer
Epigastric pain, dyspepsia, weight loss, may present with bleeding
Epigastric pain with radiation to back, weight loss, vomiting

bedside.name: NG tube aspirate
bedside.indication: Confirm upper GI source of bleeding; assess activity

laboratory.name: H. pylori testing (stool antigen or 13C-urea breath test)
CBC
laboratory.indication: Anemia from acute or chronic blood loss

laboratory.name: Hemoglobin and Hematocrit
laboratory.indication: Baseline and trend in acute bleeding

laboratory.name: Urea and Creatinine
laboratory.indication: BUN elevated in upper GI bleed (digested blood)

laboratory.name: Coagulation profile (PT/INR)
laboratory.indication: Assess bleeding risk; correct if elevated

laboratory.name: LFTs
laboratory.indication: Rule out other causes of upper GI symptoms

imaging.name: Upper GI Endoscopy (EGD)
imaging.indication: Gold standard — visualize ulcer, biopsy (gastric), therapeutic hemostasis

imaging.name: Chest X-Ray (erect)
imaging.indication: Free air under diaphragm if perforation suspected

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV contrast
imaging.indication: If perforation suspected, free air/contrast extravasation

management.protocolReference: ACG Clinical Guideline for Peptic Ulcer Disease (2023)

resuscitation: Large bore IV access ×2 if bleeding
IV fluids: Normal Saline or Hartmann's
Blood transfusion if Hb <7 or symptomatic anemia — target Hb 7-9
NG tube for lavage if upper GI bleeding
Correct coagulopathy: Vitamin K, FFP if INR >1.5
conservative: NBM initially if bleeding or perforation
IV PPI high dose: Omeprazole 80 mg bolus + 8 mg/hr infusion for 72h (bleeding ulcers)
Oral PPI (Omeprazole 20 mg BD) for non-bleeding ulcers
H. pylori eradication therapy: 14-day triple therapy
Stop NSAIDs, aspirin, and other ulcerogenic drugs

surgical.indication: Failed endoscopic hemostasis, recurrent bleeding, perforation, obstruction
surgical.bleeding_ulcer: Laparotomy, gastroduodenotomy, under-run bleeding vessel (3-0 silk figure-of-eight), ± vagotomy and pyloroplasty
surgical.perforated_ulcer: Emergency laparotomy, Graham omental patch repair, peritoneal lavage
surgical.definitive_acid_reduction: Truncal vagotomy + antrectomy or highly selective vagotomy (rarely done in modern era)

nonOperative: Endoscopic hemostasis: adrenaline injection (1:10,000) + thermal coagulation or clips

general: NG tube on free drainage — remove when output <200 mL/day
IV fluids until tolerating oral intake
diet_advancement: Day 3-5: Soft diet — mashed potatoes, porridge, yoghurt, eggs
Day 5-7: Regular diet as tolerated — avoid spicy, acidic foods
discharge_criteria: Tolerating soft diet without pain or vomiting
No evidence of rebleeding (Hb stable, no melena)

pathways.criteria: No bleeding, no perforation, no obstruction
pathways.treatment: PPI therapy (Omeprazole 20-40 mg daily for 4-8 weeks). H. pylori eradication therapy. Stop NSAIDs. Lifestyle modification.

pathways.criteria: Hematemesis, melena, hemoglobin drop, active bleeding on endoscopy
pathways.treatment: Resuscitation. Urgent EGD with endoscopic hemostasis (injection + clips/thermal). High dose IV PPI 72h. Surgical intervention if endoscopic control fails.

pathways.criteria: Acute severe abdominal pain, peritonitis, pneumoperitoneum
pathways.treatment: Emergency laparotomy. Omental patch repair (Graham patch). Thorough peritoneal lavage. IV PPI. IV antibiotics 5-7 days.

operative_templates.procedure: Graham Omental Patch Repair (Perforated Peptic Ulcer)
steps: Suction peritoneal fluid — send for culture
Harvest omental pedicle (vascularized piece of omentum)
Secure with 3-0 silk sutures through full thickness of duodenal wall ×3-4 sutures
Tie sutures gently — do not cut through tissue
Thorough peritoneal lavage with 3-5 L warm saline
Place intra-abdominal drain if significant contamination

dischargeCriteria: Tolerating soft diet without pain or vomiting
H. pylori eradication therapy prescribed and started

patient_education.explanation: A peptic ulcer is a sore in the lining of your stomach or the first part of your small intestine (duodenum). It is most often caused by a bacterial infection (H. pylori) or by painkillers (NSAIDs like ibuprofen, diclofenac). The ulcer can cause pain, bleeding, or a hole (perforation) that needs emergency surgery. Treatment includes acid-reducing medication and antibiotics to kill H. pylori.
patient_education.warningSigns: Go to the emergency department immediately if: you vomit blood or coffee-ground material, you pass black tarry stools, you have sudden severe abdominal pain that does not go away, your abdomen becomes hard and tender, you feel faint or dizzy, or you have a fever with abdominal pain.
patient_education.activity: You can resume normal activities as you feel better. Avoid heavy lifting for 4-6 weeks if you had surgery. Get adequate rest. Reduce stress where possible.
patient_education.diet: Eat regular meals — do not skip meals. Avoid very spicy foods, fried foods, and acidic foods (tomatoes, citrus). Eat a balanced diet with vegetables, fruits, whole grains, and lean proteins. Good choices: porridge (uji), rice, bananas, boiled vegetables, bread, milk. Avoid alcohol completely — especially chang'aa and busaa which can worsen ulcers. Drink plenty of water.
patient_education.medication: Take omeprazole exactly as prescribed for the full course. If prescribed H. pylori treatment: take all antibiotics exactly as directed, even if you feel better. Do not take ibuprofen, diclofenac, aspirin, or any NSAID ever again — they will damage your stomach. Use paracetamol for pain instead. If you need heart medication (aspirin), ask your doctor about adding a PPI.
patient_education.followUp: Return to clinic in 2 weeks for review. If you had a gastric ulcer, you need a repeat endoscopy in 8-12 weeks to confirm it has healed. Ensure H. pylori eradication is confirmed with a breath test or stool test 4 weeks after finishing antibiotics.

before_discharge: H. pylori eradication therapy prescribed and explained
Dietary advice: regular meals, avoid spicy/acidic foods and alcohol
Prescribe PPI for at least 4-8 weeks

after_discharge.action: Clinical review — symptom check, medication adherence
H. pylori eradication test (urea breath test or stool antigen) — off PPI for 2 weeks
Repeat endoscopy for gastric ulcer to confirm healing and biopsy scar
Final review — ensure ulcer healed, no recurrence

when_to_return: Return of epigastric pain not relieved by medication
Vomiting blood or passing black stools
Vomiting and inability to keep fluids down
